topic: Someone receives an invitation to their high school reunion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The embossed card felt like an insult in my hand, the heavy stock mocking the casualness of the event it advertised. A high school reunion. I stared at the elegant script, my jaw tight enough to crack bone. Sarah, the sender, had somehow managed to craft an invitation that felt less like a pleasant memory and more like a deliberate act of torment. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound of the tearing sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. Every glance at the date—ten years, a decade since I’d tried to escape that suffocating environment—sent a sharp, unpleasant jolt through my chest. I paced the length of the living room, my footsteps heavy and uneven on the hardwood floor, running a hand roughly through my hair until it stood up in protest. This was a waste of my time, a deliberate intrusion into a space I had painstakingly cleared. I needed to burn this invitation, to erase the very notion of having to look back at that era.

[story 2]

When the envelope slid under the door, I didn't bother to look at the return address. My fingers, cold and clammy, snatched it up, and the paper felt abrasive against my skin. I ripped it open with unnecessary violence, the sound a sharp, ragged rip in the silence of my kitchen. Inside, the details were laid out with sickening precision: names, dates, a reminder of faces I actively avoided. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, something between a growl and a choked gasp. I dropped the remnants onto the counter, the glossy paper catching the harsh overhead light. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails bit crescent moons into my palms. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent street below, feeling a burning pressure building behind my eyes. I wanted to scream, to shatter something nearby, but I forced myself to breathe, the air catching in my lungs like shards of glass. This whole affair felt like a deliberate ambush.

[story 3]

I found the invitation tucked between the bills on my desk, and the sight of it made my stomach clench. It was an affront. A reminder of years spent navigating hallways and forced camaraderie, a time I was desperate to leave behind. I picked it up, turning it over and over, the texture of the paper rough under my thumb. A deep, simmering heat rose in my neck, making the fine hairs stand on end. I slammed my palm flat onto the desk, the sudden impact rattling the ceramic mug beside it. My breathing hitched, shallow and quick. I pushed back from the desk, my shoulders rigid, every muscle coiled tight beneath my clothing. I couldn't stand the thought of attending; the prospect felt like wading through thick, stagnant mud. I needed distance. I needed to obliterate the existence of this summons before it could take root in my composure.
